logistics.lodging_strategy: Hotels and rentals abundant across Mallorca, especially in Palma and coastal areas; book early for February peak.

final_verdict.one_liner: Historic amateur stage race offering early-season Mallorca roads for dedicated road cyclists.

videos.title: How Long Can We Hold Record Climbing Pace Up Sa Calobra?
videos.description:
Sa Calobra, the famous 9.4km Mallorcan climb and renowned training ground for pro cyclists, where Tom Pidcock set an eye-watering Strava KOM time of 22:46 He maintained a 24.9km/h pace up the vicious 7% climb, but how long can Ollie and Alex hold his rapid speed? Will they get dropped early or cling on?   

00:00 Intro 
01:50 Climb start 
03:00 Wrong time
04:12 Dropped? 
05:00 Climb PB
05:35 Climb end 
05:50 Results 

Useful Links:
Everything Cycling. All In One Place 👉 https://www.globalcyclingnetwork.com
GCN+ | Every. Unmissable. Moment. 👉 https://gcn.eu/9H-
Download the GCN App for free 👉 https://gcn.eu/app
Visit the GCN Shop 👉 https://gcn.eu/gcnshop
Join the GCN Club 👉 https://gcnclub.com
GCN Training Plans 👉 https://gcn.eu/gcntp

How long could you hold the KOM pace? 👑

Watch more on GCN...
📹 The Epic Mallorca Lap 👉 https://youtu.be/wMFthWMOBEo
📹 Watch our Editor’s Choice Playlist 👉 https://gcn.eu/editorschoice
📹 Or why not check out our Features Playlist 👉 ​​https://gcn.eu/gcnfeatures 

🎵 Music - licensed by Epidemic Sound 🎵
Lost in Your Paradise - Swif7
Something More Than Friends - Ooyy
EAST WEST_WEST EAST - Ooyy

A New Day Begins - Experia

Tricked Out - Ballpoint

#gcn #cycling #roadcycling #roadbike #bike #bikes #bikelife #climbing #mallorca #record 

📸 Photos - © Velo Collection (TDW) / Getty Images & © Sprint Cycling Agency

The Global Cycling Network (GCN) is the largest online cycling channel in the world, bringing together a global community of road cyclists to celebrate everything that’s great about the world of cycling.

Our videos bring fans compelling daily content including expert tutorials, techniques, training, racing, cutting-edge bike tech, unparalleled behind the scenes event coverage, humour,  entertainment, and more.

Presented by ex-pro riders, GCN offers a uniquely qualified insight into the world of cycling, and most importantly it’s fuelled by our passionate and enthusiastic fans – everyone who makes up the GCN community. We also bring the latest and greatest tech to your attention, showcase the best places in the world to ride and get exclusive access to events and races.

Welcome to the Global Cycling Network | Inside Cycling

videos.title: Mallorca 312: What not to do! 2025 Review and Participant Guide
videos.description:
Thinking about riding the Mallorca 312? Whether you're already signed up or just cycling-curious, this video breaks down everything you need to know about the legendary Gran Fondo. From iconic climbs and feed stations to traffic jams and pacing mistakes, here’s an honest review of what went down at the 2025 Mallorca 312, plus a full guide for future participants.

✅ What you’ll learn in this video:
How the day actually unfolded (spoiler: it wasn’t smooth sailing)
Tips for first-timers riding the Mallorca 312
What to pack, how to pace, and how to fuel
What to expect at the start, on the course, and at the finish
What I’d do differently next time

Whether you’re aiming for the full 312km, the 225, or the 167 route, this guide will help you prepare smarter and avoid some common mistakes.

Plan Your Mallorca Cycling Trip:
🚴‍♂️ Bike Island Mallorca – The best bike hire and cycling services in Mallorca. Use code SAM5 for a discount!
https://www.bikeislandmallorca.com

🚌 Mallorca Cycle Shuttle – The easiest way to access the best routes on the island. They offer daily shuttles to Sa Calobra, Cap de Formentor, Andratx, and more, so you can focus on the ride, not the logistics!
https://mallorcacycleshuttle.idevaffiliate.com/104.html

Take the stress out of planning your Mallorca trip and book with Medfeel. The greatest cycling holidays on earth. https://www.medfeel.pro/landing-samkinnearcycling

Follow me on Strava here: https://www.strava.com/activities/13107912833
Follow me on Instagram here: https://www.instagram.com/samkinnearcycling

#cycling #mallorcacycling #sacalobra #mallorca312 #Mallorca312 #CyclingMallorca #GranFondo #CyclingDocumentary #MallorcaClimbs #MallorcaCyclingRoutes #CyclingMotivation #EnduranceCycling

So, hi everyone and welcome. What's

nasely in this video. Um, what I'm

give you a little bit of a what went
wrong, what went right for my own 312
this year, uh, to hopefully give you an
idea of how to better prepare um, and
have a good time when you do it next
year. So, bit of a spoiler alert there.
For any of you who haven't seen my video
of this year's event, make sure to check
that out. It should hopefully be a
really immersive um and authentic take
on what it's like to uh participate in
the 312. I'm just in survival mode at
this point. Can't be with this
anymore. Can't wait for it to end. This
sucks. Hate it. Safe to say things
didn't entirely go to plan, but I did do
it. I finished it. I'm still standing.
So, uh so yeah, hopefully I should be
able to give you some good insight. And
uh yeah, apologies for the
sunglasses. It's quite bright out here,
man.
Anyway,
so so for a quick overview for anyone
who isn't familiar with the 312, um I
expect that's very few of you. Probably
just my mom. Um hi mom. Um so the 312,
it's been going since 2010. It is one of
Europe's largest uh closed roadportives.
Takes place on the last Saturday uh of
every April and this year there were 8
and a half thousand people taking part.
The reason the 312 is so popular and
storied and you know it gets all the
hype is it's an absolute beast. It's
probably you know if not the one of the
craziest sporties on the on the
calendar. So 312 km in length. The clue
is in the name. Uh and with just under
5,000 m elevation. The uh the organizers
like to say there's over 5,000 m but my
wahoo by the end of it was saying about
4,700. So, take that with a grain of
salt. Needless to say, it's still a
beast. Um, there's a few uh distances to
choose from. So, not everyone does the
312. You can also do the 225, which I
think is the best bang for your buck
thing to do and was what I will be doing
from now on. Um, and then there's also
the 167, uh, the shortest distance.
Sells out really quickly every year. Um,
getting faster and faster every year. I
think it sold out in about six minutes
this year. So, if you want to do it,
make sure to um to book your place
early, get the the date in your diary
for registration or alternatively
uh use a third party provider like
sponsors of the channel Medfield uh who
not only do incredible bespoke uh
holidays to Morca. Uh they they also do
312 packages, hotel transfer, everything
allinone. The bonus of that is you will
actually get in a starting pen a little
bit higher up which as we will discuss
was crucial uh to my enjoyment of of
this year's event. So hopefully that
gives you everything you need to know.
Um so so the event starts super early in
the morning. Um the event starts I think
the the starting gun fires at 6:30 in
the morning. Um but you need to be in
your starting pen I think from about
half 5. So obviously that's an early
start for everyone. It's always a rush
in the morning to get everything ready.
Um and then it starts in Ply Demuro. So
that's kind of just well it's basically
Alcudia Port of Alcudia and I'm in
Porttoensa. So I was lucky enough to get
a lift over there with a family friend
called David and some other guys who are
riding over. So thanks for the lift
guys. Um yeah, got taken over
there. Got into the green starting pen.
There's a few different starting pens. I
think you got your pink uh I think you
got blue which is VIP, pink fastest
finishers and previous events, green for
sponsors and the you know holiday
providers, the one I was in. And then
you got everyone else. So you got the
Hoy Poy over in the yellow pen. Um and
yeah, so got there nice and early. Make
sure if you are doing it, you wrap up
warm or in the case of this year's
event, bring something waterproof. It
did absolutely pelt it down um probably
from about 20 minutes before the
starting gun. It did basically stop just
within that 20 minute window, but it was
freezing. Like it wasn't too cold before
the rain started and and I was
absolutely fine. I just had uh armor and
and my Gile on. But there were some
people there in skin suits and you know,
people were were shivering. Some people
took it completely the other way. Uh
that if you'll see in the footage,
there's a guy in a full kind of painters
overall looking like um some kind of
serial killer or something. He actually
did reach out and he he seemed very non
plus about the whole thing. Takes all
sorts. Um anyway, so everyone's there,
everyone's really excited. There's
always a good vibe uh before the event,
trepidation, um kind of gallows humor as
everyone is kind of very nervous for for
what's going to take place for the day.
Um, but yeah, we got off very quickly.
Um, I don't think so. So, what I try to
do with my full recap video is give an
immersive and authentic experience as I
said. And I think maybe it wasn't quite
that because I was in the green starting
pen. So, I got out probably only 5
minutes after the starting gun. Whereas
I know people who are back in yellow,
sometimes it can be another half an hour
before they cross the line. And when
they do cross that starting line, I
think it tends to be a lot busier uh
than than when I did it. Needless to
say, it is still busy when you go out.
So, be prepared for that. You know, I
think again, where I was a little bit
further forward, people tended to be
quite experienced. I know people do
complain that it's too crowded and
people don't really know what they're
doing a lot of the time, people who
haven't experienced riding in groups
before.
So, be prepared for that. And if you are
one of those people, try and get a bit
of practice before the event. You know,
you might be nervous about going to join
a group ride, but I can tell you there's
nothing bigger than a group ride of 8
and a half thousand people. So, make
sure you get that practice in um because
that can avoid some of the incidents
that do inevitably happen. So, as I
mentioned in my main video, I did not
have the best preparation. I've got some
dodgy issue with my legs. It's
been going on for a few years now. I'm
still trying to get to the bottom of it,
but it basically meant I couldn't ride
for a good couple of weeks before the
event. And it also meant that during the
day, I wasn't sure whether I was going
to have a flare up. So, it probably
wasn't until maybe 50k or so in that I
was fully confident that, yep, we're
going to do it. Legs legs are fine. Um,
so yeah, I was pretty nervous early on,
but things got off well. There was a
beautiful sunrise over Poensa, uh, Poa
Bay and Alcudia. So that was a really
nice start, especially after the rain we
had, everyone's spirits lifted. And
then, yeah, it all kind of went pretty
smoothly um, up until, you know, it
wasn't until long long late in the race
when things started going pear-shaped.
But you'll start the day with Fmenia and
you're basically riding the MA10. So up
Fmenia over towards Sacalobra and Gorg
Blau. It's around there maybe 50 km or
so in that you have the first aid
station uh of the
day. I didn't stop here because I had
plenty of water. I had loads of food
with me and I did actually have a
planned uh stop a little bit later on in
the day. From what I understand it, you
know, the aid stations get pretty pretty
chaotic.
So, apolog Jeez, 3-1 flu.
Um, yeah, it it was fine here when I was
there, but it does get much busier later
on. So, if you can plan to avoid
stopping early on, I would highly
recommend doing so. I think this is just
a water station as well rather than the
the the food that comes a little bit
later on. So, so yeah, I was the day was
kind of unfolding to plan really. Um, I
managed to get through the early climbs,
the descent off Puj Majour, kind of went
off without a hitch really. Um, all
fine. And then basically as we got
towards the end of the MA10, I started
to feel a little bit tired.
Um, and like the lack of training and
the lack of lack of miles in my legs was
maybe starting to catch up with me. So,
by the time I got to Galileo, if that's
how you say it, um, Galilee, Galileo,
Magnificico, um, yeah, I started really,
really knocking the pace off. So yeah,
in the climbs previous to that, I was
trying to join onto groups and then I'd
end up finding that they were always
going a little bit too fast for me. So I
just basically knocked it off
completely. Went up Galilea um at like
180 W just being passed by absolutely
everyone, which is fine and that's I
think probably definitely the sensible
thing to do.
Um and then as I went up Esra, so there
was a feed station before that and at
that point I swapped over to properly
having um sandwiches and cans of Coke
and stuff like that. Um as I was going
up escrow, I was still feeling pretty
crap, but there's a chap there whose
name I can't remember, but it was in the
other video. I'll put it up somewhere.
and just talking to someone really
helped me kind of get moving. Um, took
my mind off things. That is like a top
tip that I have is just if you're not

to people. The camaraderie, I think, is

and over those lumps. If you're just in

Escrow, had another sandwich, had a sit

the image. I It was very shocking. I was
just picking up
my Nutella sandwich and literally was
and I looked over and then like I mean
it was horrendous. It was horrendous and
I looked away thinking did I just see
that? Yeah. Yeah, I did. It was gross.
So anyway, you don't need to know about
that. But it was it was quite funny. Um
yeah, from there moved along quite
quickly. Spoles like left there, got in
a quick group and kind of from there got
to Allaro and and places like that. Kind
of feeling pretty good. Had a bit of
time to recover. So, by the time I got
to the 225 point, I was okay. Like I was
kind of a little bit bored of the whole
thing. Um, as you kind of do get in in
these events, but I was fine after that.
And and basically once you've got past
the 225 turning heading towards Arta,
you've done all the climbing, you've
done all the interesting riding, which
is why if you've done this event before,
like me, I'm not going to do the 312
full one again. Mark my words. Because
after that, the riding isn't that
interesting. You're just on flat, windy,
zigzaggy roads that it just goes on and
on and on and on. And I just felt like
by this point. And here we go.
Might as well. So why? What went wrong?
So I mismanaged my nutrition. Rookie
error. Normally because I'm a cheapkate,
I rely on peanut butter and jam
sandwiches. loads of Harry B.
Another sweet. And I do always have like
carb drinks in my bottle cuz you can buy
kilos of that for for napp.
So I was just fueling differently. The
reason why the day before at the expo I
met the lovely people at Meabar and I'm
sorry team at Meabar because you
probably watched back my first video and
thought what have we done wrong here?
You haven't done anything wrong. I met
them at the expo, Moran based company,
and they gave me a load of nutrition,
loads of gels, uh, little
electrolyte gel things, uh, recovery

and months and months. I can't, you
know, I can't remember the last time I

And I kind of felt a little bit unsure

But I think it's cuz I just zoked it all

know, I'm used to having a 20 gram carb

or anything like that. But like I just I
just wasn't sitting very well with me.
And where I wasn't enjoying taking the

or six. Probably actually earlier than
that. I kind of missed a little bit of
nutrition before I got on to the
sandwiches and stuff like that. So, it
just had this knock-on effect throughout

mean, I had a little bit of an uptick in

few hours I was just in an absolute
hole. And yeah, it felt felt horrible.
It felt like it was just it felt like it
was 90k to go forever, then it was 80k

um until I got to to
to here is another thing that I want to

spectators are incredible particularly

and saying that this actually

Um and yeah, it's just like the the

videos.curation_reason: Recent comprehensive review with specific insights on the Mallorca 312 experience, logistics, and practical advice for participants.

videos.title: Mallorca - M 312 (167) - 30.04.2022
videos.description: Impressionen vom Gran Fondo Mallorca 312 - Bilder und Eindrücke von der 167 km Strecke am 30. April 2022 mit Start und Ziel in Playa de Muro, Spanien.

videos.title: Mallorca - M 312 (167) - 24.10.2021
videos.description: Impressionen vom Gran Fondo Mallorca 312 - Bilder & Eindrücke von der 167 km Strecke am 24. Oktober 2021 mit Start & Ziel in Playa de Muro, Spanien.

videos.title: Mallorca 312: My Hardest Ever Ride
videos.description:
Here is my account of the 2025 Mallorca 312. Without question, the hardest ride I've ever completed!

Plan Your Mallorca Cycling Trip:
🚴‍♂️ Bike Island Mallorca – The best bike hire and cycling services in Mallorca. Use code SAM5 for a discount!
https://www.bikeislandmallorca.com

🚌 Mallorca Cycle Shuttle – The easiest way to access the best routes on the island. They offer daily shuttles to Sa Calobra, Cap de Formentor, Andratx, and more, so you can focus on the ride, not the logistics!
https://mallorcacycleshuttle.idevaffiliate.com/104.html

Take the stress out of planning your Mallorca trip and book with Medfeel. The greatest cycling holidays on earth. https://www.medfeel.pro/landing-samkinnearcycling

Follow me on Strava here: https://www.strava.com/activities/13107912833
Follow me on Instagram here: https://www.instagram.com/samkinnearcycling

#cycling #mallorcacycling #sacalobra #mallorca312 #Mallorca312 #CyclingMallorca #GranFondo #CyclingDocumentary #MallorcaClimbs #MallorcaCyclingRoutes #CyclingMotivation #EnduranceCycling

videos.curation_reason: First-person race experience from 2025 with authentic, real-time commentary throughout the challenging 312km event.

quotes.text: I'm just in survival mode at this point. Can't be with this anymore. Can't wait for it to end. This sucks. Hate it. Safe to say things didn't entirely go to plan, but I did do it. I finished it.
It's an absolute beast. It's probably you know if not the one of the craziest sportives on the calendar. So 312 km in length with just under 5,000 m elevation.

I don't know whether it's just cuz I'm towards the front where I was in the better pen, but generally everyone on the road seems pretty capable, a lot lower club riders. So, you know, everyone's got good etiquette.

key_challenges.name: Sa Calobra climb
key_challenges.description: A beautiful 9.4 km climb averaging 7% grade. Professional riders sustain 6.5 watts per kilo for around 20 minutes here, which many amateur cyclists can only maintain for 3-10 minutes.

key_challenges.name: Femenia
key_challenges.description: Early climb in the event. Riders use this as a gauge - if they make it over Femenia feeling good, it's typically a sign they're ready for the rest of the event.

key_challenges.name: Puig Major
key_challenges.description: Major climb encountered around 55km into the event after about 2 hours 10 minutes of riding.

key_challenges.name: Coll de Soller descent
key_challenges.description: Fast and technical descent that riders describe as scary, requiring careful line selection while weaving around other participants who may not hold their lines or cut across corners.

terrain_notes.text: The Sa Calobra climb averages 7% grade over 9.4km and requires sustained high power output. Professional pace is around 380 watts at 6.5 watts per kilo for the full climb.
Atmospheric conditions can include smoke from bonfires that affects air quality but creates mystical scenery around climbs.

race_day_tips.text: Keep power under 250 watts on climbs, maybe even less on longer ones, rather than getting carried away just because legs are turning over.
On technical descents, pick your line based on other riders' body language and where they appear to be heading, as many don't hold their lines or cut across corners.
Getting into a better starting pen allows you to get ahead of the main chaos and ride with more capable cyclists who have better etiquette.

additional_quotes.text: The event starts super early in the morning. The starting gun fires at 6:30 in the morning but you need to be in your starting pen from about half 5. So obviously that's an early start for everyone. It's always a rush in the morning to get everything ready.

There's a few different starting pens. You got blue which is VIP, pink fastest finishers from previous events, green for sponsors and holiday providers, and then you got everyone else in the yellow pen.

rider_intel.search_text: The Cinturon Ciclista a Mallorca 312 is a massive 312km sportive with nearly 5,000m of elevation that takes place the last Saturday of April. Key challenges include the Sa Calobra climb, a beautiful but brutal 9.4km ascent averaging 7% grade where professionals sustain 6.5 watts per kilo for 20 minutes. Other major climbs include Femenia early in the route and Puig Major around the 55km mark. The technical Coll de Soller descent requires careful line selection as riders weave around others who may not hold their lines. Starting pens are crucial - better pens mean riding with more capable cyclists and better etiquette. The event starts at 6:30am requiring arrival at starting pens by 5:30am. Pacing strategy is critical, with experienced riders recommending keeping power under 250 watts on climbs. The event sells out in minutes and attracts 8,500+ participants annually, making it one of Europe's largest closed-road sportives.
